content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here’s a deal. I’ll make you 30% faster at your job, starting today. It’ll feel amazing. In exchange, three things happen quietly in the background: the price of the deal changes whenever I want, the skills you’d need to walk away from it slowly stop working, and everyone else takes the same deal so the 30% stops being an advantage and becomes the baseline you’re merely keeping up with.
Would you sign?
You already did. We all did. And I’m not writing this to tell you to cancel your AI subscriptions, because I haven’t cancelled mine either. I’m writing it because a deal you can’t articulate is a deal you can’t negotiate, and almost nobody in our industry has actually read the terms.
Term one: the price is not the price
Let’s start with the part that’s already happening, publicly, with receipts.
In 2026, GitHub announced that Copilot plans would shift from flat-rate subscriptions to usage-based billing. The announcement thread collected nearly 900 downvotes, largely because users did the arithmetic out loud: a single serious agent-assisted coding session can consume $30 to $40 worth of tokens. A $10 monthly plan, exhausted in one sitting.
